title: Wall-Mounted Oak Coat Rack
summary: One oak board, five turned or bought pegs, mounted into studs. Ninety minutes, and it holds a winter's worth of coats.
description:
The simplest project in the catalog, and the one most likely to fail — because almost everyone hangs it on drywall anchors.

A loaded coat rack in a family entryway carries 30–50 lbs, applied as a downward yank every time someone grabs a jacket. Drywall anchors will hold for a season and then tear out, taking a chunk of your wall with them.

**Into studs. Every time.** Get that right and this is a ninety-minute project that lasts forever.

tools.note: Peg holes bored square. A hand drill with a guide block works, but the pegs will only look right if the holes are perpendicular.
Only if you're milling the board from rough stock.
A chamfer or roundover on the front edges.
If you turn your own pegs. Buying Shaker pegs is entirely reasonable.

materials.name: White oak, 4/4, 5" wide
materials.note: One nice, straight board. Quartersawn white oak shows ray fleck and looks spectacular for the money.

materials.name: Shaker pegs, 3-1/2"
materials.note: Buy them or turn them. Bought pegs are cheap and perfectly good.

materials.name: Wood screws, #10 x 3"
materials.note: 3" so they bite deep into the stud, through the drywall and the board.

materials.name: Wood plugs or buttons
materials.note: Cover the mounting screws. Turns a hardware-store look into a finished piece.

materials.name: Clear interior finish (hardwax oil or wipe-on poly)

cutList.note: 32" spans two studs at 16" on center, with a little overhang. This length is chosen by the studs, not by taste.
Tenon on the back end, sized to your drill bit — usually 1/2".

steps.title: Find the studs, THEN pick the length
steps.body:
Locate your studs first. Standard spacing is 16" on center.

**Your board's length is decided by where the studs are** — you want to hit at least two of them, ideally three. A 32" board spanning two studs with overhang is the usual answer.

Designing the board first and hoping studs turn up where you need them is exactly how coat racks end up on drywall anchors.

steps.title: Mill the board
steps.body:
Flatten, thickness to 3/4", rip to 5" wide, crosscut to length.

If you bought a surfaced board and it's flat, you can skip straight to sanding. There's no shame in that here.

steps.title: Bore the peg holes
steps.body:
Mark five evenly spaced hole positions along the centerline. Bore holes matching your pegs' tenons — usually 1/2" — about 5/8" deep.

**Perpendicular matters.** Drill press if you have one; if not, clamp a scrap block with a squared hole as a guide. A peg leaning 5° off square is instantly visible and looks like a mistake, because it is one.

steps.title: Shape the edges
steps.body:
Chamfer or round over the front edges and the ends. Even a small 1/8" chamfer takes the board from 'plank' to 'made'.

Optional: a gentle curve along the top edge. Nice touch, five extra minutes.

steps.title: Counterbore the mounting holes
steps.body:
Drill three mounting holes on the board's centerline, positioned to land on your studs.

Counterbore each one so the screw head sits below the surface and a wood plug can cover it. This is the detail that hides the hardware and makes it look intentional.

steps.title: Glue the pegs, sand, finish
steps.body:
Glue the pegs in. Sand to 220.

White oak takes hardwax oil beautifully — the ray fleck in quartersawn stock lights up. Two or three coats.

Finish before mounting. You cannot finish the back of a board that's already on the wall.
materials: Clear interior finish (hardwax oil or wipe-on poly)

steps.title: Mount it — into the studs
steps.body:
Level the board on the wall, drill pilot holes through the counterbores, and drive 3" screws **into the studs**.

Glue the plugs in over the screw heads, pare them flush when dry, touch up the finish.

Then hang a heavy winter coat on the end peg and lean on it. It shouldn't budge. If it does, you missed a stud — take it down and fix it now, not after it rips out of the wall.
